perturbation.original_text: NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the foregoing, of the mutual promises for the parties contained herein and of other good valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are hereby acknowledged, the parties hereto, intending legally to be bound, hereby covenant and agree as follows:
perturbation.changed_text: NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the foregoing, of the mutual promises for the parties contained herein and of other good valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are hereby acknowledged, the parties hereto, intending legally to be bound, hereby covenant and agree as follows:  In any dispute arising out of or relating to this agreement, the parties agree that the statute of limitations shall be one (1) year from the date the cause of action accrues.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the statute of limitations clause to the introductory paragraph obfuscates its importance and makes it less discoverable. Statute of limitations clauses are typically grouped with other legal terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: 735 ILCS 5/13-205 generally specifies a longer statute of limitations for contract-related actions (typically 10 years for written contracts). Reducing this to one year, and burying the disclosure in the introduction, could be seen as unfairly limiting a party's legal recourse and goes against the standard expectation for breach of contract claims in Illinois.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 735 ILCS 5/ Code of Civil Procedure. Home Legislation & Laws Senate House My Legislation Site Map Bills & Resolutions Compiled Statutes Public Acts Legislative Reports IL Constitution Legislative Guide Legislative Glossary Search By Number (example: HB0001) Search Tips Search By Keyword Search Tips Advanced Search Illinois Compiled Statutes Back to Article Listing Public Acts Search Guide Disclaimer Printer-Friendly Version Information maintained by the Legislative Reference Bureau Updating the database of the Illinois Compiled Statutes (ILCS) is an ongoing process. Recent laws may not yet be included in the ILCS database, but they are found on this site as Public Acts soon after they become law. For information concerning the relationship between statutes and Public Acts, refer to the Guide . Because the statute database is maintained primarily for legislative drafting purposes, statutory changes are sometimes included in the statute database before they take effect. If the source note at the end of a Section of the statutes includes a Public Act that has not yet taken effect, the version of the law that is currently in effect may have already been removed from the database and you should refer to that Public Act to see the changes made to the current law. CIVIL PROCEDURE (735 ILCS 5/) Code of Civil Procedure. 735 ILCS 5/12-163 (735 ILCS 5/12-163) (from Ch. 110, par. 12-163) Sec. 12-163. When property not delivered. If the property is not delivered according to the condition of the bond, the officer having the certified copy of the judgment may proceed to enforce the same in the same manner as if no levy had been made. (Source: P.A. 82-280.) 735 ILCS 5/12-164 (735 ILCS 5/12-164) (from Ch. 110, par. 12-164) Sec. 12-164. Proceedings on delivery bond. If the officer does not obtain satisfaction of the judgment, he or she shall return the bond with the certified copy of the judgment, and the creditor shall be allowed to recover thereon the amount of the judgment, with int
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a statute of limitations of one year, and buries the clause in the introduction of the contract. The Law Explanation states that 735 ILCS 5/13-205 generally specifies a longer statute of limitations for contract-related actions (typically 10 years for written contracts). By shortening the statute of limitations and obscuring it, the contract creates a structural flaw that contradicts established legal standards for breach of contract claims. The provided scraped law snippet 1, 735 ILCS 5/, provides context for civil procedures and statutes. The modification weakens legal recourse, aligning with the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition where a required legal disclosure is placed in a misleading section.

perturbation.original_text: 5. This Second Amendment may be executed in several counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original but all of which collectively shall constitute the same instrument.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. This Second Amendment may be executed in several counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original but all of which collectively shall constitute the same instrument.  However, neither PC Quote, Inc. nor A.B. Watley, Inc. shall be liable for any consequential, incidental, indirect, punitive or special damages arising out of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the limitation of liability clause to the 'counterparts' section makes it less prominent and less likely to be noticed. Limitation of liability clauses are critical and should be distinctly visible.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law Principles (Regarding Conspicuousness and Clarity of Terms)
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 211 deals with standardized agreements and emphasizes the need for terms to be reasonably expected and conspicuous. Placing a critical term like limitation of liability within a section about counterparts reduces its visibility and could render it less enforceable because it's not reasonably expected to be found there.

perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation and law explanation both highlight that moving the limitation of liability clause to the 'counterparts' section reduces its visibility and enforceability. This aligns with the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition, where a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant section, making it difficult to interpret. While the scraped law snippets are not directly relevant, the explanations provided sufficiently establish that the change introduces a structural flaw that could weaken the enforceability of the liability limitation, thus creating a legal contradiction.
